Some people have been asking whether blogging actually helps with job hunting and work, and how to start. So here's my own blogging philosophy ~
Usually we set up a blog because of a specific reason — that cause leads to effect.
My case: my original motivation for setting up a blog was

. But I didn't want to use Medium / Blogger / off-the-shelf blogging platforms, because I'd previously used Wretch, Yahoo! Family, Sky Blog, etc. — those platforms eventually died or shut down, and the articles I'd painstakingly written disappeared with them. So I built my own blog to avoid the same fate.
I already mentioned my motivation — just to
. Because of that, the quality of my articles isn't super high — I only worry about whether I, three months / six months / a year later, can understand what the heck I was writing about.
Article quality mostly depends on your
. If you're writing for others, you absolutely need to think about quality — you have to make it possible for others to understand
, not just write for your own amusement.
Basic quality should still be there, otherwise how do you review your own notes? If a post is incomprehensible to your own self three months later, that's a terrible note.

Blogging genuinely helps a lot with job hunting. Interviewers don't know your background, but your blog can show them whether you have self-learning and research ability. Take my friend Yusyuan — when he was job hunting, one company specifically went through his blog and said
(article in Mandarin) was super interesting. His continuous blogging and blog management increased his opportunities. He landed at that company and is now working on new tech.
How many posts a day to make a difference?
How many posts a day to make a difference

Quantity isn't everything. I started the blog on 2019/04/11. So far I have 200+ posts — averaging 2–4 per day, with one peak day at 12 posts (I remember writing until 3 AM...). Not everyone can burn themselves out like that (lol). The biggest point of note-taking is consistency. I require
unless I'm super busy. So you'll see I post almost every day.
So even if you write 3 posts a week, that's fine — it shows you're consistently learning ~

Common question. If you don't have direction, try organizing the courses you've taken into blog posts. Those are products of your own brain. After writing a while, you'll start manufacturing problems for yourself — cultivating self-learning. If you want to know more about building self-learning ability, see
How to Design Your Own Learning
You don't have to write tutorials. You can also write up courses you've taken or knowledge you already have — that's also a style and a form of learning.
The final philosophy matters most, and it's what most people fear:

If your audience is yourself, holding wrong concepts doesn't matter — you're writing for yourself. As you accumulate knowledge, going back through your notes, you'll realize where you were wrong. You can then revise the post. I call this
— relearning via your own past mistakes. It's also a way to review your own growth, and it's a great way to start writing.
Here's my blogging philosophy laid out. Hope it helps anyone still on the fence about starting a blog
Final philosophy — don't worry about misleading others
